title: Metrics-Driven Engineering at FAANG
description: How Staff+ engineers define, own, and communicate metrics for their systems at FAANG. Covers SLIs/SLOs/error budgets, business vs technical metrics, dashboard design principles, experimentation and A/B testing culture, and how SAP's contractual SLA approach differs fundamentally from FAANG's metric-driven engineering culture where teams own their own observability.

sections.title: Why Metrics Matter at Staff+ Level

description.value:
SAP measures success by: go-live date (did we deploy on time?), budget adherence (did we stay under budget?), number of transports (are we delivering change?). These are project-management metrics, not engineering metrics. They tell you nothing about whether the system is healthy, whether users are satisfied, or whether the business is achieving its goals. At FAANG, success is measured differently: system reliability (are we meeting our SLOs?), business impact (are our metrics trending in the right direction?), team health (is our velocity sustainable?).

A Staff+ engineer defines the metrics for their system. Not your manager. Not product. Not the PMO. You. If you do not define what success looks like for your system, someone else will — and they will likely define it in terms that do not capture what actually matters. Ownership of metrics is ownership of your system's narrative. When you define the metrics, you control the conversation about whether your system is winning or losing.

The Staff+ rule: Every system you own must have exactly one top-line success metric (the "north star"), three to five supporting metrics that explain the north star, and a clear definition of what "good" looks like for each. If you cannot articulate this in a single paragraph, you do not yet understand your system well enough.

Google's Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) handbook introduced the concepts of SLIs, SLOs, and error budgets — and they form the backbone of every FAANG team's relationship with reliability. Understanding this triad is non-negotiable for a Staff+ engineer.

SLI (Service Level Indicator): A raw measurement of some aspect of your system. Examples: latency p99 (the 99th percentile request latency), error rate (percentage of requests that return 5xx), throughput (requests per second), availability (percentage of requests that succeed). An SLI is a number you can query from your observability stack. If you cannot measure it, you cannot manage it.

SLO (Service Level Objective): A target threshold for your SLI, expressed over a time window. Example: "p99 latency must be under 200ms for 99.9% of requests over a rolling 30-day window." The SLO is your team's commitment to the organization. It says: this is what good looks like, and we promise to stay within this boundary.

Error Budget: The 100% minus your SLO target. If your SLO is 99.9%, your error budget is 0.1% of total requests. Over a month, you can spend this budget on errors — including errors caused by deployments, experiments, or refactors. Once the budget is spent, you stop shipping and focus on reliability. This is the mechanical heart of the SLO system: it quantifies how much risk you can take.

Example from an SAP integration hub context: Your integration hub handles 10M messages per day between SAP S/4HANA, Ariba, and SuccessFactors. Your SLI is the percentage of messages successfully delivered within 5 minutes. Your SLO is 99.95% delivery success over a 30-day rolling window. Your error budget is 0.05% of 10M messages = 5,000 failed or delayed messages per day, or 150,000 per month. Every deployment, every configuration change, every upstream system failure consumes from this budget. When your error budget is low, you stop all non-critical changes until you understand why.

SLO target</text><text x="530" y="112" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial" font-size="9" fill="#555">e.g., 0.1% of requests</text><text x="530" y="127" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial" font-size="9" fill="#555">Spend on deploys & experiments</text><rect x="110" y="170" width="400" height="85" rx="10" fill="#fce4ec" stroke="#c62828" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="310" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#c62828">The SLO Contract</text><text x="310" y="213" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial" font-size="10" fill="#333">Error budget exhausted → Freeze all deployments</text><text x="310" y="229" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial" font-size="10" fill="#333">Error budget remaining → Velocity is permitted</text><text x="310" y="245" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial" font-size="10" fill="#333">Tradeoff: reliability vs velocity, explicitly managed</text><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6" fill="#555"/></marker></defs></svg>

Technical metrics tell you if your system is healthy. Business metrics tell you if your business is healthy. A Staff+ engineer connects the two. This is the difference between being a senior engineer (who can tell you that p99 latency is 180ms) and a Staff+ engineer (who can tell you that every 10ms increase in latency costs the business $500K per year in conversion).

Technical metrics: Latency (p50, p95, p99), error rate (5xx percentage, 4xx percentage), throughput (requests/second, messages/minute), saturation (CPU, memory, disk, connection pool usage), availability (uptime percentage). These are the raw signals that tell your team whether the system is operating within its design parameters.

Business metrics: Revenue (daily active revenue, monthly recurring revenue), conversion (percentage of users who complete target action), retention (percentage of users who return after first visit), engagement (sessions per user per day), customer acquisition cost (CAC). These tell your stakeholders whether the company is winning.

The Staff+ skill is mapping technical metrics to business outcomes. When you can say "our p99 latency increased from 120ms to 180ms because we added a new integration connector, which means 2% fewer users complete their procurement flow, which costs the business approximately $1.2M annually," you are speaking the language that executives understand. You are not asking for budget to fix latency — you are asking for budget to recover revenue.

value.title: SLO Target: 99.9% vs 99.99%
value.description: The cost-implication tradeoff of choosing a more aggressive SLO for a system processing 10M requests/day.

rows.label: Best for
rows.99.9pct: Internal tools, batch systems, dev environments

description.value:
Every team at FAANG owns a dashboard for their system. It is the first thing looked at during oncall handoff, the first thing shared during a incident review, and the first thing questioned during a quarterly review. Dashboard design is an engineering skill. A bad dashboard creates noise. A great dashboard tells a story.

Principle 1 — Show the SLO first. The single most important question about any system is: is it meeting its SLO? The top-left position of your dashboard must answer this question immediately. Use a single big-number widget: "SLO: 99.92% (target: 99.9%)" with a green/yellow/red status indicator. If the viewer has to scroll or click to find out whether the system is healthy, the dashboard has failed.

Principle 2 — Show trends, not just snapshots. A single datapoint is useless. Show the 7-day and 30-day trend for every key metric. Is latency increasing week over week? Is throughput decreasing? Trends tell you about trajectory before it becomes a crisis.

Principle 3 — Show anomalies, not everything. The human brain can process about three to five visual signals at once. If your dashboard has 20 charts, nobody will read any of them. Use anomaly detection or alerting to surface only what needs attention. Everything else should be one click away in a deeper drill-down.

Principle 4 — Reduce signal-to-noise ratio ruthlessly. Every chart on your dashboard should have a clear audience and a clear action associated with it. If you cannot explain what someone should do after looking at a chart, remove it. Noise hides signal.

FAANG companies make decisions through experiments, not opinions. If someone believes that changing the button color from blue to green will improve conversion, they do not argue about it — they run an A/B test. If an engineer believes that switching from synchronous to asynchronous processing will improve throughput, they do not debate it in a design review — they run an experiment with a small percentage of traffic and measure the result.

Experimentation culture: Every significant change to a system should be tested and measured. This includes infrastructure changes, not just UI changes. At Netflix, engineers run experiments on CDN routing algorithms. At Google, search ranking changes go through a rigorous experimentation pipeline. At Amazon, every deployment is an experiment: canary deployments measure error rates and latency before rolling out to 100% of traffic.

SAP engineers face a fundamental shift here. SAP environments are used to deterministic decision-making: you analyze requirements, design the solution, implement it, test it in a sandbox, and transport it to production. The assumption is that if you analyzed correctly and tested thoroughly, the change will work as expected. FAANG uses probabilistic decision-making: you hypothesize, you experiment, you measure the outcome, and you accept that some experiments will fail. The question is not whether a change will work — it is whether you can detect failure quickly enough and roll back before significant impact.

As a Staff+ engineer, you are responsible for creating the experimentation infrastructure for your team. This means: feature flags for gradual rollouts, metric instrumentation before the change ships, defined success and failure criteria, automated rollback triggers, and a culture where failed experiments are celebrated (you learned something) rather than punished (you caused an incident).

sections.title: SAP vs FAANG: SLA to SLO Mindset Shift

description.value:
This is the most fundamental mindset shift an SAP engineer must make when transitioning to FAANG. In the SAP world, reliability is governed by contractual SLAs (Service Level Agreements). You negotiate an SLA with the customer: "We guarantee 99.5% uptime." If you violate it, you pay a contractual penalty — often a percentage of the contract value. The SLA is a legal document. It is enforced by lawyers and contracts. The goal is to meet the SLA exactly and no more, because exceeding it costs money without generating revenue.

At FAANG, reliability is governed by aspirational SLOs (Service Level Objectives). Your team defines the SLO: "We target 99.9% availability for this service." If you violate it, there is no contractual penalty. There is no lawsuit. There is a conversation: your team burned through its error budget. You stop shipping new features. You focus on reliability. You understand why the budget was consumed and you prevent it from happening again. The SLO is not a legal contract — it is a team commitment. It is enforced by culture and accountability, not by lawyers.

What this means for you as a Staff+ engineer:

  • You choose your SLO. You decide what target makes sense for your system based on business requirements, not based on what the contract says. This is empowering but also terrifying — if you choose wrong, your team will either waste money on over-engineering or suffer frequent outages.
  • You manage your error budget. You decide when to spend it on velocity (ship that refactor) and when to conserve it for reliability (hold the line). Every deployment is a conscious draw from the budget.
  • You accept tradeoffs explicitly. A 99.99% SLO costs 3-5x more in infrastructure and engineering than a 99.9% SLO. If the business does not need 99.99%, do not build for it. Make the tradeoff explicit and document it.

The hardest part of this shift is giving up the certainty of a contract. An SLA tells you exactly what you need to deliver. An SLO asks you to decide what to deliver and to own the consequences of that decision. This is the essence of Staff+ ownership.

details: Staff+ Insight: The shift from SLA to SLO is one of the hardest conceptual changes for SAP engineers. An SLA is a contract with a penalty. An SLO is a commitment that your team chooses, with an error budget that your team manages. It gives you the freedom to trade reliability for velocity — as long as you stay within your error budget. This is not about lowering standards; it's about making explicit tradeoffs. Define your first SLO within your first 90 days. It will force you to understand what your system does and what success looks like.
